instruction: Corrige el siguiente fragmento que usa `output.assets.contains(policy_id, asset_name)` para verificar un NFT en Aiken v3.
input: Código incorrecto: `output.assets.contains(policy_id, "Token")`. Proporciona la versión correcta con `assets.has_nft`.
output:
El método `contains` no existe sobre `Value` en la stdlib de Aiken v3. La forma correcta es usar `assets.has_nft` del módulo `cardano/assets`:

```aiken
use cardano/assets

```

Además, la propiedad correcta del output para acceder al valor es `.value`, no `.assets`.

instruction: Explain what `transaction.find_input` returns when the output reference is not found, and how it differs from `transaction.resolve_input`.
input: Focus on Option vs. failure semantics in the context of Aiken v3 validators.
output:
`transaction.find_input(inputs, ref)` returns `Option<Input>`: `Some(input)` if found, `None` if not. The caller must handle the `None` case explicitly, usually with `expect Some(...)` which fails the validator if absent.

`transaction.resolve_input(inputs, ref)` is a direct unwrap variant that immediately fails script evaluation when the reference is not found — no `Option` wrapping. Use `find_input` when you want to branch on absence; use `resolve_input` when absence is always invalid and you want concise code.

instruction: Explica cómo funciona `interval.contains` en Aiken v3 y da un ejemplo de uso con `validity_range`.
input: Respuesta técnica corta orientada a validadores de tiempo.
output:
`interval.contains(range, point)` devuelve `True` si el punto entero `point` está dentro del intervalo `range`. En un validador, `self.validity_range` representa el rango de tiempos POSIX en milisegundos dentro del cual la transacción es válida según el nodo. Un uso típico es verificar que un momento específico cae dentro de ese rango:

```aiken
interval.contains(self.validity_range, datum.unlock_at)
```

Esto garantiza que la transacción solo es aceptada cuando el slot actual es compatible con `unlock_at`.

instruction: Explain the security risk of checking `self.extra_signatories` without binding the check to a specific key stored on-chain.
input: Give a short explanation with a concrete attack scenario.
output: If a spend validator only checks that `self.extra_signatories` is non-empty, any transaction with any signature satisfies the condition. An attacker can add their own key as an extra signatory and drain the script UTxO. The safe pattern is to store the authorised key (e.g. a `VerificationKeyHash`) on-chain in the datum, then verify `list.has(self.extra_signatories, datum.owner)`. This binds the signature check to a specific key committed at datum creation time.

instruction: Explica por qué en Aiken v3 el handler `spend` recibe `own_ref: OutputReference` en lugar de resolver automáticamente la propia entrada.
input: Respuesta técnica orientada al modelo eUTxO.
output: En el modelo eUTxO, una transacción puede gastar múltiples UTxOs del mismo script al mismo tiempo. Si el runtime inyectase automáticamente "la propia entrada", no estaría claro a cuál de ellas referirse. Al pasar `own_ref: OutputReference`, el sistema garantiza que el validador sabe exactamente cuál de las entradas corresponde a la invocación actual. El código debe llamar a `transaction.resolve_input(self.inputs, own_ref)` para obtener esa entrada concreta, lo que hace explícita la asociación entre la ejecución del script y el UTxO que se está gastando.

To query how many tokens of a specific policy and asset name were minted, use `assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name)`. instruction: Explain the difference between `transaction.find_input` and `transaction.resolve_input` in Aiken v3.
input: Focus on return type and failure behavior.
output: `transaction.find_input(inputs, output_reference)` returns `Option<Input>`, so it never fails — callers must handle both `Some` and `None`. `transaction.resolve_input(inputs, output_reference)` returns `Input` directly, failing the script if the reference is not found in the list. In a spend validator you typically use `resolve_input` with `own_ref` because the own input must always exist; `find_input` is preferable when absence is a valid outcome you want to branch on.

input: Refer to the Transaction type documented in Aiken v3. Keep it short and technical.
output: `self.id` is the `TransactionId` of the transaction being validated. It is the hash of the serialised transaction body. In practice it is useful in one-shot minting policies: you can parameterise the validator with a specific `OutputReference` and verify that `self.id` matches the hash derived from consuming that UTxO, guaranteeing the policy can only fire once for that particular spend. Outside one-shot patterns, `self.id` is rarely checked directly inside a validator.

instruction: Correct the following invalid Aiken v3 pattern: `list.any(self.inputs, fn(i) { i.value.get_ada() > 0 })`.
input: Explain what is wrong and provide the corrected version using documented stdlib helpers.
output:
Two things are wrong. First, `get_ada()` does not exist in Aiken's stdlib; it is an invented method. Second, `i` is an `Input`, so the value lives at `i.output.value`, not `i.value`. The correct pattern uses `assets.lovelace_of`:

```aiken
use cardano/assets
use aiken/collection/list

list.any(self.inputs, fn(i) { assets.lovelace_of(i.output.value) > 0 })
```

instruction: Explain why `transaction.resolve_input` will cause the script to fail rather than returning None when the input is not found.
input: Compare the failure semantics of resolve_input versus find_input in Aiken v3.
output: `transaction.find_input` returns `Option<Input>`, so a missing UTxO gives `None` and the caller must handle both branches. `transaction.resolve_input` is a total function that expects the UTxO to exist; if it is absent the evaluation fails immediately (equivalent to calling `expect Some(...)` on a `None`). In a spend handler the own_ref is always present in the inputs list by construction, so `resolve_input` is safe to use there. In other contexts where presence is not guaranteed, prefer `find_input` and handle the `None` branch explicitly.

instruction: Explica la diferencia entre `self.inputs` y `self.reference_inputs` en el contexto de un validador de minteo en Aiken v3.
input: Respuesta orientada a cuándo usar cada uno dentro de una política de minteo.
output: En una política de minteo, `self.inputs` contiene las UTxO que la transacción consume. Si el minteo necesita que se gaste una UTxO específica (por ejemplo, para un one-shot mint), se consulta `self.inputs`. `self.reference_inputs` contiene UTxO que la transacción solo lee, sin consumirlas. Si el minteo necesita consultar datos como un precio, una lista de permitidos o un NFT de autorización que no debe desaparecer, se usa `self.reference_inputs`. La elección define si la UTxO de contexto sobrevive o no a la transacción.

instruction: Correct this anti-pattern: using `output.value.lovelace` in an Aiken v3 validator to check Ada in an output.
input: Show the broken code snippet and then the corrected version using cardano/assets.
output:
Broken:
```aiken
// WRONG — lovelace is not a field on Value
let ada = output.value.lovelace
```

Corrected:
```aiken
use cardano/assets

let ada = assets.lovelace_of(output.value)
```

`Value` in Aiken v3 is an opaque multi-asset map. Ada is accessed through the `assets.lovelace_of` helper, not a struct field.

instruction: Explain why `self.mint` in a spend validator can be used to assert no tokens were minted in the transaction.
input: Cover the type of self.mint and how to check it is empty. Be concise.
output: In Aiken v3, `self.mint` is a `Value` present in every transaction context, including spend handlers. It represents all tokens minted or burned in the transaction. To assert nothing was minted or burned, you can check that the value equals the empty asset map. The idiomatic check is `self.mint == assets.zero` (or equivalently verify `assets.lovelace_of(self.mint) == 0` and no other policies exist). instruction: Explain the eUTxO rationale for passing own_ref as a separate argument to the spend handler rather than deriving it from self.inputs.
input: Why does the ledger inject own_ref explicitly instead of making the validator search self.inputs itself?
output: In the eUTxO model a validator may be executed multiple times in a single transaction if several UTxOs locked by the same script are spent simultaneously. Each execution corresponds to a different UTxO being unlocked. Without the explicit `own_ref` argument the validator would have no unambiguous way to know which of those UTxOs it is currently validating without additional context. By injecting `own_ref` the ledger guarantees that each execution receives the exact `OutputReference` of the UTxO under validation. The validator can then call `transaction.resolve_input(self.inputs, own_ref)` to inspect only its own UTxO rather than making assumptions about list order or searching heuristically, which would be unsafe.

instruction: Explain what happens on-chain if an Aiken v3 spend validator uses `list.find` to scan all inputs for an NFT instead of resolving its own output reference.

instruction: Correct this hallucination: `assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name) > 0` is used to detect minting but the developer assumes this also covers burning.
input: Explain why a positive-only check misses the burn case and show the right condition for 'no change to supply'.
output:
`quantity_of` returns a signed integer. Minting produces a positive value; burning produces a negative value. A check of `> 0` therefore only blocks minting while silently allowing burns. To assert that no tokens of that asset are minted or burned in the transaction, the correct condition is:

```aiken
assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name) == 0
```

If the intent is to allow both minting and burning but never a zero-change entry, use `!= 0`. Match the predicate to the actual invariant the script needs to enforce.

instruction: Explain why `transaction.resolve_input` is preferred over manually calling `list.find` on `self.inputs` when locating the script's own UTxO.
input: Cover correctness, failure semantics, and code clarity.
output: `transaction.resolve_input(inputs, own_ref)` is a single documented call that finds the input matching the given `OutputReference` and unwraps it, failing the script immediately if the reference is absent. Manual `list.find` returns an `Option`, forcing an extra `expect Some(...)` unwrap at the call site and risking a silent `None` if the wrong reference is passed. Using `resolve_input` also signals intent clearly — this is the validator's own UTxO — whereas a manual search obscures that semantic. For the own-input lookup, `resolve_input` is both safer and more readable.

instruction: Explica cuándo usar `interval.contains` frente a `interval.is_entirely_after` en validadores de Aiken v3.
input: Contexto: restricciones de tiempo en rangos de validez. Respuesta técnica y breve.
output:
`interval.contains(range, point)` comprueba si un instante concreto cae dentro del rango de validez, sin exigir que todo el rango esté en un lado del punto. Es útil cuando quieres saber si una fecha específica (por ejemplo, el momento de desbloqueo) está dentro de la ventana declarada.

`interval.is_entirely_after(range, point)` es más estricto: exige que el extremo inferior del rango sea estrictamente mayor que el punto. Esto garantiza que *ningún* instante del rango de validez es anterior al límite, lo que es la forma correcta de impedir que una transacción se ejecute antes de un deadline.

En resumen: usa `contains` para "el rango incluye este momento" y `is_entirely_after` para "la transacción no puede haberse construido antes de esta fecha".

instruction: Explain why using `list.head(self.inputs)` to locate your own script input is unsafe in Aiken v3.

An attacker can construct a transaction where the target script's UTxO is not first, causing the validator to inspect the wrong input. The safe pattern in Aiken v3 is to use the spend handler's `own_ref: OutputReference` together with `transaction.resolve_input(self.inputs, own_ref)`, which pinpoints exactly the UTxO being consumed.

Calling `assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name)` on it returns `0` for any policy and asset name. This means a guard like `assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, name) == 0` evaluates to `True` in a no-mint transaction, which is the correct way to assert that a spend validator is not accompanied by any minting activity under a specific policy.

instruction: Explain the difference between `transaction.find_input` and `transaction.resolve_input` return types in Aiken v3.
input: Focus on Option<Input> vs Input and what happens when the OutputReference is not found.
output:
`transaction.find_input(inputs, output_reference)` returns `Option<Input>`. If no input matches the reference it returns `None`, allowing the caller to handle the missing case explicitly.

`transaction.resolve_input(inputs, output_reference)` returns `Input` directly. If no input matches, the validator fails immediately — it is equivalent to `expect Some(i) = find_input(...)`. Use `resolve_input` when you are certain the reference must be present (e.g., inside a `spend` handler where `own_ref` is always in `self.inputs`) to keep code concise. Use `find_input` when absence is a valid branch that needs handling.

instruction: Explica cómo se usan los campos `datums` y `redeemers` del contexto de transacción en Aiken v3 para coordinar múltiples scripts.

output:
`self.datums` es un mapa de `DatumHash` a `Data` que contiene todos los datums presentados en la transacción, incluyendo los de entradas que no son el script actual. `self.redeemers` es un mapa de `ScriptPurpose` a `Data` con los redeemers de todos los scripts invocados en la transacción.

Un caso de uso práctico es la coordinación entre un validador de gasto y una política de acuñación: el validador de gasto puede verificar que el redeemer del mint en la misma transacción tiene un valor concreto accediendo a `self.redeemers`, sin necesidad de parámetros adicionales. Esto permite contratos multi-script que se validan mutuamente sin duplicar lógica.

instruction: Explain why `assets.lovelace_of` must be used instead of accessing a `lovelace` field directly on a `Value` in Aiken v3.

Ada is stored under an empty policy id and an empty asset name, not as a named field. There is no `.lovelace` field exposed on `Value`. `assets.lovelace_of(value)` is the documented stdlib accessor that queries the correct key in that map. Writing `value.lovelace` or `output.value.lovelace` will not compile because those fields do not exist on the type.

instruction: Explain why `self.mint` is empty (zero) when no minting or burning occurs in a transaction.
input: Focus on the eUTxO model and what the field represents in a transaction that only moves UTxOs.
output: In Cardano's eUTxO model, `self.mint` captures the net asset delta introduced by minting policies in the current transaction. When no mint or burn action is included, no policy script runs and no new tokens are created or destroyed. The ledger represents this as an empty Value — a Value with no policy entries. In Aiken v3, `self.mint` will therefore be `assets.zero` in such transactions. Checking `self.mint == assets.zero` inside a spend validator is a valid way to assert that the transaction does not touch any tokens under minting policy control.

A diferencia de `transaction.find_input`, que devuelve `Option<Input>` y permite manejar el caso vacío, `resolve_input` asume que la referencia existe y provoca un fallo irrecuperable si no es así. Por eso se usa principalmente cuando el propio validador conoce con certeza que la referencia está presente, como ocurre con `own_ref` en un handler `spend`.

instruction: Explain the security risk of using `list.head(self.inputs)` to obtain the script's own input in a spend validator.
input: Focus on transaction ordering and multi-input attacks.
output: Using `list.head(self.inputs)` assumes the script's UTxO is always the first input in the list. This assumption is unsafe because the ordering of inputs in a Cardano transaction is determined by the ledger's sorting of `OutputReference` values (by transaction hash and index), not by the order the script author expects. An attacker can craft a transaction that places a different UTxO before the script's UTxO, so `list.head` resolves to the wrong input. The safe pattern is to use `own_ref` from the `spend` handler together with `transaction.resolve_input` or `transaction.find_input`, which directly locates the exact UTxO being validated regardless of its position in the list.

instruction: Explica qué es `self.datums` en Aiken v3 y cómo se usa junto con `transaction.find_datum`.
input: Respuesta técnica corta. Incluye un ejemplo de uso.
output:
`self.datums` es un mapa de `DatumHash` a `Data` incluido en el contexto de transacción de Aiken v3. Contiene los datums que se adjuntaron explícitamente como witneses en la transacción (datums hasheados, no inline). `transaction.find_datum(datums, hash)` busca en ese mapa y devuelve `Option<Data>`. Se usa cuando un output tiene su datum referenciado por hash en lugar de inline:

```aiken
use cardano/transaction

```

Si el datum es inline, se accede directamente desde `output.datum` sin necesitar este mapa.

instruction: Explain why quantity_of returns a negative number when tokens are burned in Aiken v3.
input: Focus on the semantics of Transaction.mint and how minting/burning is represented.
output: In Aiken v3, `self.mint` is a `Value` that records the net change in token supply for the current transaction. Minting increases supply, so the quantity is positive. Burning decreases supply, so the quantity is stored as a negative integer. When you call `assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name)`, a result of `-1` means exactly one token of that asset is being burned in this transaction. This is consistent with how the Cardano ledger represents multi-asset deltas.

instruction: Explain what the `id` field of a Transaction represents in Aiken v3 and when it is useful.
input: Short technical explanation, oriented to common validator patterns.
output: `self.id` in an Aiken v3 transaction handler is the `TransactionId` — the hash that uniquely identifies the transaction on-chain. It is useful in one-shot minting policies, where you can bake a specific UTxO's output reference (which includes the transaction id of the transaction that produced it) into the policy at compile time. By checking that the UTxO with that exact `TransactionId` and output index is consumed in `self.inputs`, you guarantee the policy can fire at most once, because a UTxO can only be spent once.

instruction: Explica la diferencia entre `self.datums` y los datums en línea (`inline datum`) en Aiken v3.
input: Respuesta técnica breve orientada al modelo eUTxO.
output: En Aiken v3, `self.datums` es un mapa que asocia hashes de datum con sus datos correspondientes. Estos datums se adjuntan a la transacción de forma explícita cuando se usa el modelo de datum por hash: el UTxO almacena solo el hash y el dato completo viaja en el cuerpo de la transacción. Los datums en línea, en cambio, se almacenan directamente en el UTxO dentro del campo `output.datum`. No requieren `self.datums` para resolverse; simplemente se accede a `output.datum` y se hace pattern matching. La función `transaction.find_datum` sirve para buscar en `self.datums` por hash cuando no se usa el modelo en línea.

instruction: Corrige este fragmento de código Aiken v3 que usa `output.assets.contains` para comprobar la presencia de un token.

output: `output.assets.contains(policy_id, asset_name)` es una expresión inventada que no existe en la stdlib de Aiken v3. La forma correcta es usar `assets.quantity_of(output.value, policy_id, asset_name) > 0` o `assets.has_nft(output.value, policy_id, asset_name)` si se trata de un NFT. El campo correcto del output es `.value`, no `.assets`, y las comprobaciones de presencia se realizan a través de funciones del módulo `cardano/assets`.

instruction: Explain the security risk of using `list.head(self.inputs)` to get the script's own input in a spend validator.
input: Describe the attack vector and the correct mitigation.
output: Using `list.head(self.inputs)` assumes the script's UTxO is the first input, but Cardano does not guarantee input ordering from the validator's perspective. An attacker can construct a transaction where the script UTxO is not first, causing `list.head` to return a different input. The validation logic then runs against the wrong UTxO, potentially passing checks it should not. The correct approach in Aiken v3 is to use the `own_ref: OutputReference` argument provided by the `spend` handler and resolve it with `transaction.resolve_input(self.inputs, own_ref)`. This anchors all checks to exactly the UTxO being validated.

instruction: Explain what the `id` field on a Transaction represents in Aiken v3 and when it is useful.
input: Focus on its type and practical use cases in smart contract logic.
output: The `id` field on a `Transaction` in Aiken v3 is a `TransactionId`, which is a hash uniquely identifying the transaction on-chain. It is rarely used in validator logic directly, but it is valuable for one-shot minting policies that must verify a specific UTxO is consumed — the consumed UTxO's `OutputReference` includes its originating `TransactionId`. Another use case is cross-script coordination where one script needs to assert it is running in the same transaction as another by matching transaction ids derived from redeemers or datums.

instruction: Corrige el error: un desarrollador usa `self.mint.lovelace` para verificar que no se mintea ADA en un validador de gasto.
input: Explica por qué no existe ese campo y cuál es la forma correcta en Aiken v3.
output:
`self.mint.lovelace` no existe en Aiken v3. El tipo `Value` es opaco y no expone campos directamente. Además, el protocolo de Cardano prohíbe mintear o quemar ADA, por lo que `self.mint` nunca contendrá lovelace. Si el objetivo es verificar que no se mintea ningún token de una política específica, la forma correcta es:

```aiken
assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name) == 0
```

Para verificar que el campo mint está completamente vacío, se puede comparar contra `assets.zero`.

instruction: Explain why using `list.head(self.inputs)` to get the script's own input is unsafe in Aiken v3 spend validators.
input: Address the multi-input transaction attack vector.
output: Cardano transactions can include multiple inputs in arbitrary order. `list.head(self.inputs)` simply returns the first input in the list, which is not guaranteed to be the UTxO currently being validated. An attacker can construct a transaction where a cheap, attacker-controlled UTxO appears first in `self.inputs`, satisfying any check that relies on the head element. The correct approach is to use the `own_ref: OutputReference` provided to the `spend` handler and resolve the exact input with `transaction.resolve_input(self.inputs, own_ref)`. This anchors the check to the specific UTxO the validator governs.

instruction: Explain the difference between `self.datums` and inline datums on outputs in Aiken v3.
input: Keep the explanation short and practical for contract authors.
output: `self.datums` is a map from `DatumHash` to `Data` attached to the transaction witness set. It provides datums by their hash so scripts can look them up with `transaction.find_datum`. Inline datums, by contrast, are embedded directly in an output via the `InlineDatum` constructor on the output's `datum` field. They require no hash lookup — the script can pattern-match on the output's datum directly. Use `self.datums` when the datum is supplied off-chain as a witness; use inline datums when the datum lives on-chain at the UTxO itself.

instruction: Explica por qué `assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name) > 0` no cubre el caso de quema en un validador de minteo en Aiken v3.
input: Contexto: el validador quiere permitir solo minteo positivo y rechazar quemas.
output: Cuando se quema un token, su cantidad en `self.mint` es negativa. La condición `> 0` falla para quemas, lo cual puede parecer correcto, pero el peligro está en que un validador que solo verifica una política concreta podría seguir ejecutándose en una transacción de quema donde esa condición simplemente no aplica. Si el validador es la única política en el campo `mint`, una quema haría que `quantity_of` devuelva un valor negativo, y `> 0` la rechazaría correctamente. Sin embargo, si la intención es ser explícito, es mejor comprobar `== 1` o `>= 1` según el caso de uso, y si se quiere también prohibir quemas expresamente, agregar `&& quantity_of(...) >= 0` o simplemente usar `== N` con el valor esperado exacto.

instruction: Explain what happens if `transaction.resolve_input` is called with an OutputReference that does not appear in the inputs list in Aiken v3.
input: Contrast with find_input behavior. Focus on on-chain failure semantics.
output: `transaction.resolve_input(inputs, ref)` fails (aborts script execution) if no matching input is found. It is equivalent to calling `transaction.find_input` and then using `expect Some(...)` on the result. `transaction.find_input` returns `Option<Input>`, so the caller can handle the `None` case gracefully. In practice, `resolve_input` is the right choice when the input must exist for the transaction to be valid — a missing input means something has gone fundamentally wrong and the validator should reject. Use `find_input` when the absence is a legitimate case that requires branching logic.

instruction: Explain what the `account` argument in an Aiken v3 withdraw handler represents and how it differs from `self`.
input: Keep the explanation short and focused on the v3 handler signature.
output: In the Aiken v3 withdraw handler `withdraw(redeemer, account, self)`, `account` is the `StakeCredential` associated with the withdrawal being validated — it identifies which staking credential is authorising the reward withdrawal. `self` is the full `Transaction` context and gives access to inputs, outputs, mint, extra_signatories, and every other transaction field. The distinction matters when a single script manages multiple stake accounts: `account` lets the validator know which specific credential is being acted upon, while `self` provides the shared transaction-level view.

instruction: Explain the difference between `self.inputs` and `self.reference_inputs` when writing a mint policy in Aiken v3.
input: Focus on which field to use when you need to check an authorisation NFT without spending it.
output: In a mint policy, `self.inputs` lists the UTxOs being consumed by the transaction. `self.reference_inputs` lists UTxOs that are read but not spent. When you want to authorise a mint based on an NFT that should remain on-chain after the transaction — for example a governance or membership token — you should check `self.reference_inputs`, not `self.inputs`. Checking `self.inputs` would mean the NFT is being consumed, which is almost never the intent for a persistent authorisation token.

instruction: Explica por qué `assets.quantity_of` devuelve un valor negativo al quemar tokens en Aiken v3.
input: Contexto: el campo `self.mint` en el manejador `mint` refleja el saldo neto de minteo.
output: El campo `self.mint` representa el delta neto de tokens para esa política en la transacción: valores positivos indican minteo y valores negativos indican quema. Cuando una transacción destruye tokens, la cantidad neta es negativa porque se eliminan unidades del suministro circulante. `assets.quantity_of(self.mint, policy_id, asset_name)` devuelve ese mismo delta, por lo que una quema aparece como un número negativo. Por eso, para verificar que solo se permite quemar y no mintear, se comprueba que el resultado sea estrictamente menor que cero.

instruction: Explain what happens when `transaction.find_input` is called with an OutputReference that does not exist in the inputs list.
input: Contrast the result with transaction.resolve_input under the same condition.
output:
`transaction.find_input(inputs, ref)` returns `Option<Input>`, so when the reference is not found it returns `None`. The caller must handle that case explicitly, typically with a `when` expression or `expect Some(...)` that will fail the validator if the match is absent.

`transaction.resolve_input(inputs, ref)` has no `Option` wrapper. It directly returns an `Input` or aborts execution if the reference is not present. This makes it concise when you are certain the input must exist — such as when resolving `own_ref` in a spend handler — but it offers no recovery path if the input is genuinely missing.

instruction: Corrige este error: un desarrollador escribe `assets.has_nft(output.assets, policy, name)` para verificar un NFT en Aiken v3.
input: El campo correcto de un `Output` para acceder al valor es `output.value`, no `output.assets`.
output:
El campo `assets` no existe en el tipo `Output` de Aiken v3. El campo correcto es `value`. La llamada correcta es:

```aiken
assets.has_nft(output.value, policy, name)
```

Usar `output.assets` genera un error de compilación porque ese campo no está definido en la estructura `Output`. Siempre se accede al bundle de tokens a través de `output.value`.

instruction: Explica el propósito del campo `id` en una transacción de Aiken v3 y cómo se usa en el patrón one-shot.
input: Respuesta técnica y breve.
output: El campo `self.id` en Aiken v3 es el hash de la transacción en curso (`TransactionId`). Es único por construcción del protocolo: ninguna otra transacción confirmada puede tener el mismo identificador. En el patrón one-shot, se utiliza para garantizar que un token solo puede mintearse una vez: el nombre del asset se construye a partir del hash de una UTxO específica que debe ser consumida, vinculando de forma irreversible la política a ese gasto único. Alternativamente, algunos diseños registran `self.id` como parte del datum para anclar el estado inicial del contrato a una transacción irrepetible.
